title: Intersight Server Power and Thermal Telemetry
spl:
index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:metrics" metric_name IN ("power_draw_watts","inlet_temp_celsius","exhaust_temp_celsius")
| timechart span=1h avg(metric_value) as avg_val by server_name, metric_name
| where avg_val > case(metric_name="inlet_temp_celsius", 28, metric_name="exhaust_temp_celsius", 45, metric_name="power_draw_watts", 800)
description: Power draw and thermal readings across the fleet feed capacity planning, detect cooling anomalies, and correlate with workload spikes. Trending helps predict rack-level power budget exhaustion.
value: Power draw and thermal readings across the fleet feed capacity planning, detect cooling anomalies, and correlate with workload spikes. Trending helps predict rack-level power budget exhaustion.
implementation: Enable metric collection in the Intersight Add-on. Set thresholds per server model. Alert on thermal exceedances or power draw approaching PDU circuit limits.
visualization: Line chart (power/thermal over time), Heatmap (server x temperature), Single value (peak power draw).
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Intersight Add-on`.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `cisco:intersight:metrics` (power, temperature).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Enable metric collection in the Intersight Add-on. Set thresholds per server model. Alert on thermal exceedances or power draw approaching PDU circuit limits.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=cisco_intersight sourcetype="cisco:intersight:metrics" metric_name IN ("power_draw_watts","inlet_temp_celsius","exhaust_temp_celsius")
| timechart span=1h avg(metric_value) as avg_val by server_name, metric_name
| where avg_val > case(metric_name="inlet_temp_celsius", 28, metric_name="exhaust_temp_celsius", 45, metric_name="power_draw_watts", 800)
```

Understanding this SPL

**Intersight Server Power and Thermal Telemetry** — Power draw and thermal readings across the fleet feed capacity planning, detect cooling anomalies, and correlate with workload spikes. Trending helps predict rack-level power budget exhaustion.

Documented **Data sources**: `cisco:intersight:metrics` (power, temperature). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Intersight Add-on`. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: cisco_intersight; **sourcetype**: cisco:intersight:metrics. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=cisco_intersight, sourcetype="cisco:intersight:metrics". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `timechart` plots the metric over time using **span=1h** buckets with a separate series **by server_name, metric_name** — ideal for trending and alerting on this use case.
• Filters the current rows with `where avg_val > case(metric_name="inlet_temp_celsius", 28, metric_name="exhaust_temp_celsius", 45, metric_name="power_draw_…` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.


Step 3 — Validate
Compare with Cisco Intersight (Alarms, audit, and inventory) for the same object types and time range.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Line chart (power/thermal over time), Heatmap (server x temperature), Single value (peak power draw).
grandmaExplanation: Power draw and thermal readings across the fleet feed capacity planning, detect cooling anomalies, and correlate with workload spikes — so hardware faults and capacity problems do not take services down.
knownFalsePositives: Cluster events during planned hardware refresh, capacity adds, or scheduled maintenance modes.
